name: TypeORM
what_it_does: TypeScript/JavaScript ORM supporting ActiveRecord and DataMapper patterns with decorator-based entity definitions, migrations, and relations for SQL databases.
use_cases: Define database entities with @Entity/@Column decorators and generate TypeScript types alongside schema
Run and manage schema migrations via CLI to evolve database structure in agent workflows
Query related entities using eager or lazy loading with OneToMany/ManyToMany/ManyToOne relations
Use Repository or EntityManager pattern to perform CRUD operations without writing raw SQL
Scaffold a data layer for an agent-managed application with PostgreSQL, MySQL, or SQLite
not_for: NoSQL databases — TypeORM is SQL-only despite some MongoDB support being incomplete
Production auto-synchronization — synchronize:true will drop columns and is dangerous outside development
Ultra-high-performance bulk inserts — use raw SQL or a query builder like knex for batch operations
best_when: Building TypeScript applications where schema-as-code and type-safe entity relations are more important than raw query flexibility.
avoid_when: You need fine-grained SQL control, run in an environment without decorator support, or are operating at bulk-insert scale where ORM overhead matters.

auth.notes: Library — authentication is handled via database connection string or DataSourceOptions config object.

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: Errors often surface as raw database driver messages; TypeORM wraps them inconsistently — agents must parse driver-level text.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: Upsert via .save() is idempotent only if primary key is known; findOrCreate patterns require manual implementation.
known_agent_gotchas: synchronize:true silently drops columns on entity change — never enable in production or CI environments an agent controls
Lazy loading relations returns Promises but looks like property access — agents must await or use eager loading to avoid undefined
Migration generation diffs against the current database state, not source control; running on a dirty DB produces wrong migrations
EntityManager and Repository share a transaction only if the same QueryRunner is passed explicitly — easy to miss in agent-generated code
Circular relation definitions (e.g., bidirectional OneToMany/ManyToOne) can cause infinite recursion in JSON serialization

security_score_components.security_notes: Connection credentials live in DataSourceOptions — agents must not log or serialize this object. Parameterized queries are used by default but raw() bypasses protection.
